questions.question: Julius Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March — which date is that?
questions.explanation: Julius Caesar was stabbed to death on March 15, 44 BC in the Theatre of Pompey by a group of senators including Brutus and Cassius. 'Ides' referred to the 15th in March, May, July, and October; the 13th in other months. Caesar had reportedly been warned to beware the Ides of March by a soothsayer.
questions.memory_hook: Ides of March = March 15. Caesar was warned, ignored it. 23 stab wounds from 60 conspirators. Shakespeare made the date immortal.

questions.question: The ancient Aztec capital of Tenochtitlan was built on an island in the middle of Lake Texcoco. It was destroyed by Spanish conquistadors and is now the site of which modern city?
questions.explanation: Tenochtitlan, founded around 1325 CE, was the capital of the Aztec Empire and one of the largest cities in the world at the time of Spanish arrival. Hernán Cortés and his forces conquered and destroyed it in 1521. Mexico City was built on its ruins, and remnants of Aztec architecture can still be found beneath the modern city.
questions.memory_hook: Tenochtitlan = Aztec capital on a lake, now Mexico City.

questions.question: The Korean War armistice was signed in 1953, effectively creating which boundary between North and South Korea?
questions.explanation: The Korean War armistice, signed on July 27, 1953, established the Korean Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) near the 38th parallel as the boundary between North and South Korea. The 38th parallel had been used as a temporary division after WWII when the Soviet Union occupied the north and the United States occupied the south. The war ended in an armistice — not a peace treaty — so technically the war has never officially ended.
questions.memory_hook: Korea divided at the 38th parallel. No peace treaty, just a ceasefire. Technically still at war since 1953. The DMZ is one of the most fortified borders on Earth.

questions.question: Which empire controlled most of the known world during the Pax Romana — the period of relative peace from 27 BC to 180 AD?
questions.explanation: The Pax Romana ('Roman Peace') lasted approximately 206 years, from the reign of Augustus (27 BC) to the death of Marcus Aurelius (180 AD). During this period, the Roman Empire was the dominant power in the Western world, covering about 5 million square kilometers and ruling an estimated 70 million people — about 21% of the world's population at the time.
questions.memory_hook: Pax Romana = Roman peace, 27 BC to 180 AD. 200 years of relative stability across the known world. Rome was so dominant that it didn't need to fight major wars — the peace was enforced by the threat of Roman legions.

questions.question: In golf, what is the rarest standard scoring term — completing a hole in just one stroke, typically only possible on a short par-3 hole?
questions.explanation: An ace (also called a hole in one) occurs when a player sinks the ball from the tee in a single stroke. It is most common on par-3 holes. The odds of an amateur golfer making an ace are approximately 1 in 12,500. Professionals manage one roughly every 3,000 attempts.
questions.memory_hook: Ace = hole in one. One stroke, one shot. Most celebrated moment in golf.

questions.question: In which sport would you find terms like 'birdie,' 'eagle,' and 'bogey'?
questions.explanation: These scoring terms are all from golf. A birdie is one stroke under par, an eagle is two under par, and a bogey is one over par. An albatross (or double eagle) is three under par — extremely rare. Par is the expected number of strokes for a skilled golfer to complete a hole. The bird theme: the rarer the bird, the more impressive the score.
questions.memory_hook: Birdie, eagle, bogey = golf. One under, two under, one over. Golf borrowed birds for its scoring language. Hole-in-one on a par-3 = albatross, the rarest and most majestic bird in the scoring system.

questions.question: In rugby union, what term describes the temporary removal of a player from the field for 10 minutes after receiving a yellow card for foul play?
questions.explanation: The sin bin in rugby union requires a yellow-carded player to leave the field for exactly 10 minutes, leaving their team with 14 players. A red card results in permanent dismissal. The term 'sin bin' is also used in ice hockey, where penalties lead to players sitting in a physical box beside the ice.
questions.memory_hook: Sin bin = 10 minutes off for yellow card in rugby. Borrowed by ice hockey too.

questions.question: Which band released the 1979 album 'The Wall,' featuring the hit 'Another Brick in the Wall'?
questions.explanation: The Wall (1979) by Pink Floyd was a concept album written largely by Roger Waters, exploring themes of isolation, abandonment, and mental breakdown. The album spawned a massive live concert production and a 1982 film. 'Another Brick in the Wall (Part II)' — with its iconic chorus sung by schoolchildren — was banned in South Africa during the anti-apartheid movement.
questions.memory_hook: The Wall = Pink Floyd, 1979. Roger Waters wrote most of it about his own isolation and his father killed in WWII. The schoolchildren's chorus was banned in South Africa. A protest song disguised as a rock anthem.

questions.question: Who directed The Godfather (1972), widely considered one of the greatest films ever made?
questions.explanation: Francis Ford Coppola directed The Godfather (1972), based on Mario Puzo's novel, starring Marlon Brando, Al Pacino, James Caan, and Robert Duvall. The film won three Academy Awards including Best Picture and is consistently ranked as one of the greatest films in cinema history. It spawned two sequels. Coppola was only 32 when he made it.
questions.memory_hook: The Godfather = Francis Ford Coppola, 1972. Marlon Brando's Don Corleone, Al Pacino's Michael. Coppola was 32. The studio didn't want Brando or Pacino — both turned out to be perfect.

questions.question: Which artist holds the record for the most Grammy Awards won in history?
questions.explanation: Beyoncé holds the record for the most Grammy wins in history. She set the single-night record at the 2024 Grammy Awards and continued adding to her total at the 2025 ceremony with wins for her Cowboy Carter album. She is the most decorated artist in Grammy history.
questions.memory_hook: Beyoncé = 32 Grammy wins, most ever. She broke the all-time record in 2023, surpassing a classical conductor who held it for decades. She spans pop, R&B, country, and everything between.

questions.question: Which sea lies between Europe and Africa and is almost entirely enclosed by land?
questions.explanation: The Mediterranean Sea is bordered by Southern Europe to the north, North Africa to the south, and the Middle East to the east. Its only natural connection to the Atlantic Ocean is through the Strait of Gibraltar. It has been a crucial crossroads of civilization for thousands of years — the Romans called it 'Mare Nostrum' (Our Sea).
questions.memory_hook: Mediterranean = almost landlocked. Surrounded by Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. Only one narrow opening to the Atlantic. The cradle of Western civilization — Greece, Rome, Egypt, Phoenicia all rose around its shores.

questions.question: What is the name of the process by which plants lose water through their leaves?
questions.explanation: Transpiration is the process by which water moves through a plant from roots to leaves and evaporates into the air through tiny pores called stomata. A single large tree can transpire hundreds of liters of water per day. The Amazon rainforest transpires so much water that it generates its own rainfall — the forest creates the rain that sustains it.
questions.memory_hook: Transpiration = plants sweating. Water moves up from roots, exits through leaf pores into the air. The Amazon forest transpires so much water it rains on itself — the trees make their own weather.
